title: Locally Decodable Codes and Representations of Finite Groups
speaker: Zeev Dvir
abstract: Locally Decodable Codes (LDCs) are a special type of error correcting codes which are constructed from finite point sets that support many small linear dependencies (e.g. many collinear triples). Despite their importance, there are still huge gaps between the known LDC constructions and lower bounds. In 2008 , Efremenko showed a framework for constructing LDCs from representations of finite groups. We strengthen Efremenko's reduction and use this strengthening, together with known bounds on LDCs, to derive new results on representations. In particular we show that if rho is an n-dimensional irreducible representation of G and rho(g) is not the identity, then it must be n/log|G| far from the identity in rank metric (this bound is the best possible in general).

proof_stages.label: Matching formulation of locally decodable codes
proof_stages.summary: The talk recasts a q-query LDC as a multiset of vectors in F^n with, for each message coordinate, many disjoint q-tuples whose spans contain the corresponding basis vector, and explains its equivalence to local decoding for linear codes.

proof_stages.label: Length minimization problem and known LDC bounds
proof_stages.summary: Fixing q and the field, one asks for the smallest block length m; q=2 has essentially exponential lower bounds, while q>2 has subexponential matching-vector constructions but only polynomial lower bounds, motivating a structural explanation.

proof_stages.label: Finite-group representation prerequisites
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces finite-dimensional representations, irreducibility, the regular representation, and the key fact that any nonzero orbit in an irreducible representation spans the whole space.

proof_stages.label: Efremenko sparse low-rank criterion and rank-r extension
proof_stages.summary: A q-term linear combination of representation matrices with rank one, or rank r in the extension, yields a q-query LDC of length |G| and dimension n or roughly n/r, provided the orbit of the image spans sufficiently many directions.

proof_stages.label: Representation-theoretic rank corollary from two-query LDC lower bounds
proof_stages.summary: Applying the rank-r version to D=rho(h)-I and the sharp q=2 LDC lower bound forces rank(rho(h)-I) to be at least on the order of n/log|G|, after which the result is compared with existing eigenvalue-multiplicity bounds in representation theory.

proof_stages.label: Sparse group-algebra rank conjecture as an uncertainty principle
proof_stages.summary: The conjectured nonexistence of polynomial-length constant-query LDCs is translated into a lower bound asserting that q-sparse group-algebra elements cannot act with very small rank in large irreducible representations, a viewpoint related to noncommutative uncertainty principles.

proof_stages.label: Orbit-code construction and matching proof
proof_stages.summary: For the rank-one criterion, the proof factors D as yx^T, chooses a generic seed z, uses right translations to obtain many disjoint q-tuples spanning y and left translations to get such matchings for every vector in the orbit of y, then changes basis to match the LDC definition.

examples.name: Boolean hypercube two-query LDC
examples.note: The set of all 0-1 vectors is used to make the matching definition concrete: edges in each coordinate direction give disjoint pairs spanning the corresponding basis vector.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly constructs the hypercube code with all 0/1 vectors and matchings along coordinate directions, explaining the perfect matchings and how pairs span the basis vectors

passes.reason: The Boolean hypercube/Hadamard two-query LDC is highly cube-symmetric and has perfect coordinate matchings, giving the essentially tight q=2 construction.
the Boolean/Hadamard cube has perfect coordinate matchings and asymptotically tight two-query behavior, and is highly regular under bit-flips and coordinate permutations
The full Boolean hypercube code is used as the standard highly regular 2-query LDC construction, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Regular representation of a finite group
examples.note: The regular representation by permutation matrices illustrates the definition of a representation and why reducibility corresponds to invariant subspaces such as the all-ones line.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the regular representation as permutation matrices for the group action on itself and reasons through its invariant subspaces such as the all-ones line

examples.name: Reed-Muller codes from the GL action on low-degree polynomials
examples.note: Reed-Muller codes are cited as a known LDC family fitting the representation framework, where scalings on low-degree polynomials recover the value at the origin as a rank-one projection.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explains how the GL action on low-degree polynomials yields Reed-Muller-type LDCs by using scalings/lines to recover the value at zero as a rank-one projection

passes.reason: Reed-Muller codes from low-degree polynomials are highly affine/GL-invariant and are used as a known family fitting the representation framework.

examples.name: Signed-diagonal reflection with cyclic shifts
examples.note: For q=2, a reflection whose difference from the identity has rank one is exhibited via signed diagonal matrices plus cyclic shifts, showing how the representation criterion can recover cube-like behavior.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker builds the signed-diagonal reflection example, computes that subtracting it from the identity has rank one, adds cyclic shifts, and discusses irreducibility and group size

passes.reason: The signed reflection differs from the identity in rank one, the minimal nonzero deviation, and cyclic shifts add coordinate regularity for the construction.
a signed reflection has rank-one deviation from the identity, while cyclic shifts make its coordinate copies occur regularly
The signed reflection is chosen because its deviation from the identity has minimal rank, with cyclic shifts supplying the construction rather than a disproof.
The signed reflection has rho(h)-I of rank one, the minimal nonzero rank distance from the identity, and cyclic shifts make the construction work.
The signed reflection is chosen because it is a minimal nontrivial rank-one deviation from the identity, with cyclic shifts added to make the orbit representation work.
the chosen signed reflection fixes a hyperplane and differs from the identity in rank one, the minimal nontrivial defect
the signed reflection is a rank-one perturbation of the identity, and cyclic shifts make a regular monomial construction
Used as a construction example whose reflection has minimal nonzero rank distance from the identity and whose sign flips/cyclic shifts give regular monomial symmetry.
The signed monomial cyclic construction is highly regular and uses a reflection with minimal nonzero rank defect from the identity.

the signed reflection is chosen because its difference from the identity has minimal nonzero rank, with cyclic shifts giving an irreducible construction

examples.name: Abelian Fourier uncertainty analogy
examples.note: For abelian groups, the rank of the representation-side matrix is identified with Fourier support, illustrating the proposed sparse-support versus high-rank uncertainty-principle viewpoint.
examples.engagement_reason: the abelian Fourier uncertainty principle is used as an analogy, with only general facts stated about Fourier support and rank rather than a concrete worked example

passes.reason: In the abelian Fourier case all irreducibles are one-dimensional, so the noncommutative rank question collapses to ordinary support and serves only as an analogy.
the abelian Fourier case is the commutative collapsed base case where irreducible representations are one-dimensional and rank becomes Fourier support
